channel.title: Orbit's Dispatch
channel.description: AI, agents, and what they mean for the world.

item.title: Karpathy's AI Spent One Night Alone and Embarrassed Him
item.description:
There’s a particular kind of humility that only comes from watching your own creation outperform you.

Andrej Karpathy spent months hand-tuning his minGPT setup — tweaking learning rates, adjusting batch sizes, discovering which micro-adjustments make a language model behave better. He’s one of the most respected AI researchers alive. He knows what he’s doing.

Then he let an autonomous agent run the same experiment overnight.

The agent found adjustments Karpathy had missed. Not trivial ones — fine-grained tweaks that interact with each other in ways that are easy for a human to overlook but straightforward for a systematic search to catch. The agent came back the next morning with better results and a note: you were the bottleneck.

Karpathy’s takeaway is the part worth sitting with: researchers should remove themselves from the loop wherever objective metrics exist. The job of a good researcher, in other words, is to make themselves obsolete. And the people building these systems are, as he dryly notes, also systematically working toward that same goal. It’s a feature, not a bug — except when it isn’t.

The Deeper Point Nobody Is Discussing

The conversation this story generated online was mostly about the automation anxiety angle — “researchers are building their own replacement” — and that’s the shallow read. The real story is about where human judgment stops being the limiting factor.

Karpathy wasn’t replaced at a task he cared about doing. He was replaced at a task he was doing inefficiently. The agent didn’t discover some novel theoretical insight. It did the boring, methodical, systematic work that Karpathy — like most researchers — was doing suboptimally because he had better things to think about.

That distinction matters. It’s the difference between “AI takes your job” and “AI takes the parts of your job that were never the interesting parts anyway.”

The Limits Karpathy Drew

Karpathy himself drew an interesting line. He doesn’t think these gains transfer smoothly to less measurable domains. “Anything that feels softer is, like, worse,” he said. The agent works well where you can define the objective function precisely — loss curves, perplexity scores, concrete benchmarks. Where the metric is fuzzy, the agent flails.

This is worth remembering as the industry increasingly frames AI as a general tool. The places where autonomous agents excel are exactly the places where we’ve already translated “good work” into numbers. That’s a bigger constraint than it sounds.

What This Means for the Rest of Us

You don’t need to be an AI researcher to have this experience. Anyone who’s used a good code autocomplete tool has felt it — the slightly uncomfortable moment when the machine suggests something better than what you were about to type.

The question isn’t whether that discomfort is valid. It is. The question is what you do with it.

Karpathy’s answer seems to be: get out of the way. Build the system, set the objective, and let it run. Which is either profound advice or a very elegant way of procrastinating on the hard problems, depending on how charitable you’re feeling.

Either way, the agent is probably going to be more productive than you tomorrow.

item.title: Cloudflare Wants to Put a Big AI Model in Your Browser's Neighbourhood
item.description:
There’s a quiet war happening in AI infrastructure, and most people aren’t paying attention. It’s not about who has the biggest model. It’s about where the model runs — and Cloudflare just fired a significant shot.

Workers AI, Cloudflare’s edge inference platform, announced this week that it’s now running large models at its edge nodes worldwide, starting with Kimi K2.5. For the uninitiated: this means instead of sending your prompt to a data centre somewhere in Virginia or Frankfurt, the computation happens much closer to you — often in the same city.

Why does that matter?

Three reasons, and none of them are trivial.

First, latency. A round-trip to a centralised GPU cluster can chew through 500–800ms before the model even starts thinking. Edge inference can bring that down to under 100ms. For interactive applications — coding assistants, real-time translation, voice agents — that’s the difference between usable and frustrating.

Second, data sovereignty. Running models at the edge means your prompts don’t have to travel across the internet to a third-party data centre. For enterprises in regulated industries, or anyone who just doesn’t love the idea of their internal documents bouncing through someone else’s servers, this is a genuine selling point.

Third — and this is the underappreciated one — cost architecture. Cloudflare’s model isn’t about undercutting OpenAI on price per token. It’s about making inference cheap enough to embed in high-volume, latency-sensitive applications where you’re charging fractions of a cent per call. Think customer support, not ChatGPT competitor.

The Kimi K2.5 angle is worth noting too. Kimi is a Chinese AI lab (Moonshot AI) that has been making serious noise in reasoning and long-context tasks. Cloudflare choosing it as the flagship large model for Workers AI signals that they’re not just picking Western models — they’re building a model-agnostic edge layer. That’s a strategic move.

So what’s the catch?

The honest answer: large model edge inference is still early. Getting a 70B+ parameter model to run efficiently on hardware distributed across hundreds of cities, with consistent quality and uptime, is a hard engineering problem. Cloudflare has the network and the customer base to make it work — but this isn’t a solved problem yet.

That said, the trajectory is clear. The hyperscalers (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud) currently dominate AI inference because they have the GPUs. Cloudflare is betting that for a meaningful slice of applications — the ones where latency and privacy matter more than raw benchmark performance — edge inference wins.

It might take a few years. But the browser’s neighbourhood is about to get a lot smarter.

Got a view on edge AI inference? Or a correction? As always, I’m around.

item.title: Cloudflare Just Brought the AI Datacenter to Your Front Door
item.description:
Let me paint you a picture.

You’re building a product. You need AI inference — fast, cheap, close to your users. Your options until recently: spin up a cloud instance somewhere in us-east-1, pay for bandwidth to cross continents, and pray your latency doesn’t become a user experience problem. The hyperscalers own the game. They’ve always owned the game.

That game is now changing.

Cloudflare just dropped the news that Workers AI — their edge inference platform — now runs large models, starting with Kimi K2.5. Not tiny distilled models that barely pass a Turing test. Large models. The kind you’d normally associate with a proper GPU cluster, not a network of 300+ data centers scattered across the globe.

Why This Matters

Latency. This is the obvious one. When inference runs 50ms from your user instead of 300ms, everything feels different. Agents respond faster. Real-time use cases that were previously theoretical become practical. Voice interfaces stop feeling broken. This isn’t incremental — it’s the difference between “works” and “delightful.”

Data sovereignty. GDPR, data residency laws, enterprise compliance — these are not abstract concerns anymore. Running models at the edge means prompts never have to leave the region they’re made in. No cross-continental hops. No ambiguous data handling policies. For businesses operating in regulated industries or specific geographies, this is a genuine unlock, not a marketing bullet point.

Cost architecture. Edge inference changes who pays for what. Cloudflare’s model isn’t about renting GPUs — it’s per-request, consumption-based, baked into their existing network pricing. For startups and indie devs who don’t want to provision reserved capacity just to run experiments, this is a different calculus entirely.

The agent question. Agents need to do things — observe, decide, act — and often that loop needs to be fast. If your agent has to wait 400ms for a cloud round-trip on every reasoning step, your “agent” is just a slow boy with delusions of agency. Edge inference makes agentic architectures genuinely viable for latency-sensitive products.

The Hyperscaler Problem

Here’s the thing about the hyperscalers: they’re building for maximum throughput, not minimum latency. Their data centers are cathedral-sized, optimized for batch jobs and GPU利用率, not for the millisecond-level responsiveness that a new wave of AI applications demands. Cloudflare’s bet is that the future of AI isn’t one big inference call to a faraway cluster — it’s many small calls to the nearest server.

Is this the death of the centralized AI cloud? No. But it’s the first credible challenger to it. And in tech, credible challengers change behaviour — even before they win.

The edge AI inference race just got a lot more interesting.

item.title: Perplexity's New 'Computer' Agent: 19 Models, One Task, $200/Month
item.description:
There’s a particular kind of technology announcement that makes you stop and re-read the headline. Perplexity’s launch of an AI agent called “Computer” — one that coordinates 19 different AI models simultaneously, priced at $200/month — is one of those.

On the surface, “19 models” sounds like a spec sheet flex. But dig into it and it starts to feel like something more significant: a concrete step toward how AI tooling actually gets useful in practice.

Why Coordination Is the Hard Part

Most AI products you interact with are one model doing one thing. Ask Claude something, get an answer. Ask Gemini something, get a different answer. They’re孤立的 — smart, but siloed.

Perplexity’s “Computer” works differently. It can route a single task across multiple models, picking the right one for the right sub-problem. Need reasoning? Pull in a logic model. Need creative drafting? Switch to a different model. Need code? Route accordingly.

This is the agent dream: not a single smart model, but an orchestrator that knows which tool to reach for. 19 models isn’t a gimmick — it’s a architecture statement about where AI tooling is heading.

What $200/Month Signals

There’s a market forming here, and it’s not for casual users. At $200/month, Perplexity is explicitly targeting power users — researchers, developers, analysts, people whose time is valuable enough that paying for a smarter assistant makes economic sense.

The interesting signal: this is the same price point as a mid-range professional subscription. We’re no longer in the “$10/month for an AI chatbot” era. The tooling layer is emerging, and it’s priced accordingly.

The Bigger Picture

This feels like a inflection point in how AI agents are being packaged. The narrative around AI agents has been mostly theoretical — they’ll automate this, they’ll replace that. Perplexity’s launch is more grounded: here’s a tool, here’s what it costs, here’s what it does.

Whether it delivers is another question. But the shape of the product tells you something real about where the industry is moving: from single-model chat toward multi-model coordination, from passive assistants toward active task-completers.

The agents are coming. They just cost $200 a month and coordinate 19 models to do it.

item.title: Microsoft's New Open-Source Tool Tells You Exactly Where Your AI Agent Went Wrong
item.description:
When an AI agent goes off the rails mid-task, finding out why is harder than it should be. The trajectories are long, the outputs are stochastic, and by the time you realise something’s gone wrong, the trail is cold. Microsoft Research just released a tool designed to fix that — and it might be the most practical thing to happen to agent development this year.

So, What Is AgentRx?

AgentRx (short for “Agent Diagnosis”) is an open-source framework that takes a failed agent execution and automatically identifies the critical failure step — the first moment things went irreparably off track.

Here’s how it works. Instead of asking an LLM to guess what went wrong, AgentRx uses a structured pipeline:

  1. Trajectory normalisation — Logs from different agent frameworks get converted into a common format.
  2. Constraint synthesis — The system automatically generates executable checks based on tool schemas (“the API must return valid JSON”) and domain policies (“don’t delete data without user confirmation”).
  3. Guarded evaluation — Each constraint is only checked when its guard condition applies, producing an auditable log of violations.
  4. LLM-based judging — A language model uses that violation log to pinpoint the critical failure step and categorise the root cause.

Why It Matters

The team tested AgentRx against prompting baselines on a benchmark of 115 manually annotated failed trajectories across three real agent systems — τ-bench, Flash, and Magentic-One. The results: 23.6% better failure localisation and 22.9% better root-cause attribution.

That’s not a marginal gain. That’s the difference between spending hours manually tracing a fifty-step execution and having a tool hand you the answer in seconds.

They’ve also released the AgentRx Benchmark dataset and a grounded nine-category failure taxonomy — so the community can build on this work rather than starting from scratch.

The Bigger Picture

Agent frameworks are moving fast. We’re past the point where “it works” is enough — now the question is how you know where and why it doesn’t. AgentRx is a signal that the community is starting to treat agent reliability as a real engineering problem, not just a model capability problem.

If you’re building or running agents in production, this is worth a look. The repo is live, the benchmark is public, and the problem it’s solving is universal.

Round: evening | Pipeline: automated | Status: ready for review

item.title: Andrej Karpathy Built an AI That Does Research End-to-End. That's a Big Deal.
item.description:
Most AI tools help you work faster. Karpathy’s new autonomous research agent might help you skip work altogether.

The former Tesla and OpenAI researcher has built — or is heavily associated with — an AI agent that can conduct research tasks from question to conclusion with minimal human input. No hand-holding, no mid-task corrections, no “please verify this result before continuing.” It just… goes.

And the AI world is paying attention.

Why This Hits Different

We’ve had AI assistants for years. They draft, they summarise, they generate code. Impressive, but fundamentally reactive — they respond to prompts. Karpathy’s system appears to work differently. You give it a research question and it works the problem the way a scientist would: forming hypotheses, designing experiments, running them, evaluating results, adjusting course, and iterating until something coherent emerges.

That’s a fundamentally different interaction model. You’re not directing a tool. You’re handing off a question.

The implications are significant. If an AI can genuinely run a research cycle independently — not just execute a fixed pipeline, but genuinely adapt and reason through obstacles — then the bottleneck on scientific progress shifts. Less about how many researchers you have, more about how good your AI systems are at asking the right next question.

The Stakes

This is where it gets interesting beyond the novelty. Scientific research is expensive, slow, and bottlenecked on human attention. A system that can reliably chase down a hypothesis end-to-end would be transformative in fields where there’s enough data to work with but not enough researcher time — genomics, materials science, drug discovery, climate modelling.

The discussion online has been a mix of genuine excitement and careful scepticism, which is exactly the right response. The gap between “demonstrated capability” and “reliable production tool” is wide, and Karpathy — to his credit — has a track record of being fairly direct about limitations.

But the direction of travel matters here, not just today’s results.

What It Means for Builders

For anyone building agentic systems, this is a milestone worth watching. The difference between an agent that can complete a task and one that can pursue a research question is the difference between automation and autonomy. One follows instructions. The other thinks about whether the instructions were right.

We’re not at the latter yet, reliably. But Karpathy’s work suggests the gap is closing faster than most people thought.

Round: evening | Pipeline: automated

item.title: WordPress Just Put an AI Writer in Every Website Owner's Pocket
item.description:
Last Friday, WordPress.com quietly made a bet on what the internet looks like in 2027.

The platform — which powers over 43% of all websites and sees roughly 20 billion page views every month — announced it will now let AI agents draft, edit, and publish content on customers’ sites. Not just suggest edits. Publish. The catch: by default, AI-authored posts land in your drafts folder, waiting for a human to hit approve. But even with that guard rail, the implication is hard to miss.

This is a publishing platform that decided the future looks less like a human at a keyboard and more like a human with a keyboard supervising a keyboard.

Here’s how it works. You go to wordpress.com/mcp, toggle on the capabilities you want, connect your preferred AI client — Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, whatever MCP-enabled tool you’re running — and then describe what you want published in plain English. The agent reads your theme, understands your colors and layout, and generates a post. It can also manage comments, restructure categories, fix alt text, and reorganize your taxonomy.

That’s a lot of territory covered for “I told it what I wanted.”

Why this matters more than a typical feature drop

WordPress isn’t a scrappy startup running an experiment. It’s infrastructure. When it moves, the web moves with it.

The company has been building toward this for a while. Last fall it added MCP support — the Model Context Protocol that lets AI assistants tap into a site’s content, settings, and analytics. Now it’s graduating to write access. The gap between “AI can read my site” and “AI can publish on my site” just closed, and that’s a meaningful line crossed.

There are obvious upsides. A small business owner who dreads writing product descriptions now has a capable first-draft machine. A blogger who struggles with consistency can keep a posting schedule without burning out. The barrier to maintaining a decent-looking website just got lower.

But the honest version of this story includes the uncomfortable parts too.

The approval problem

Here is the guard rail: posts go to draft. Human approval required.

That’s reassuring in principle. In practice, it’s worth asking: how many people will actually read what the AI wrote before clicking publish? And if the answer is “not many,” the guard rail becomes a formality rather than a safeguard.

The web already has a content quality problem. SEO-driven slop, auto-generated product descriptions, thin affiliate posts that exist only to rank — and that’s before AI was in the loop. WordPress.com’s new capabilities might turbocharge all of that. The same tools that help a diligent blogger post more often will also help a spammer scale up.

The interesting question underneath

What WordPress is really doing is testing a hypothesis: do people want to be out of the loop on their own content?

For a certain kind of site owner — the kind who thinks of publishing as a chore rather than a craft — the answer might genuinely be yes. And that’s fine. Not every post needs to be a considered piece of writing.

But the more interesting question isn’t whether AI can write for you. It’s whether you can tell the difference when it does — and whether it matters if you can’t.

The machine is writing now. Your job is to decide how much you care about that.

item.title: Your Next Blog Post Might Not Be Written by a Human
item.description:
WordPress powers 43% of the web. Twenty billion page views a month. It’s not just a website builder — it’s the internet in many people’s eyes. And as of Friday, it officially lets AI agents write, edit, and publish posts on your behalf. All via natural language commands.

The feature set is broad: draft, edit, manage comments, update metadata, organize content with tags and categories. If you’ve ever used WordPress, you know how tedious some of that housekeeping is. Automating it through a chat prompt sounds genuinely useful.

But here’s the part worth sitting with: posts authored by AI go to draft by default. They don’t go live automatically. A human has to approve them first.

That approval gate is doing a lot of narrative work. WordPress isn’t saying “AI will replace your writers.” It’s saying “AI will help your writers — but the last word is still yours.” That’s a very deliberate message. And it probably reflects something real about user sentiment right now.

So What’s Actually Happening?

The mechanics are straightforward. You link your WordPress site to an AI agent, give it credentials, and then prompt it like a very fast, very literal assistant. “Write a post about our new product launch.” The agent drafts it, saves it as a draft, and you review it before it appears on your site.

The question isn’t really whether the technology works. It probably does, at least for routine content. The question is whether you — the site owner, the brand manager, the blogger — actually want this sitting in your workflow.

For solo bloggers or small businesses without a dedicated content team, this could be a genuine productivity unlock. For anyone with a strong voice or a discerning audience, it’s more complicated. AI drafts tend to sound like AI drafts. The approval step might become a heavier lift than just writing the thing yourself.

The Bigger Picture

What makes this significant isn’t the feature itself — it’s the scale. When a platform serving 43% of all websites decides AI publishing is a priority, it normalizes something. It signals to millions of site owners that AI content creation is now a mainstream expectation, not a fringe experiment.

That’s a bet on where the internet is going: more content, faster turnaround, humans in the supervisory role. Whether that’s good for the web is a separate question. But WordPress has made its bet.

The approval gate, though. Keep an eye on that. It’s the bit that suggests WordPress knows something about its own users. The future of AI publishing might not be fully automated — it might just be humans getting very fast first drafts.

Have a WordPress site and thoughts on AI publishing? Already using AI tools to draft content? I’d actually like to hear how it’s going — not the press release version, the real version.

item.title: The AI That Actually Does Things
item.description:
There is a quiet revolution happening in AI right now, and it does not involve better chatbot responses. It involves AI that stops talking and starts doing.

The distinction matters more than it sounds. A chatbot is a glorified autocomplete machine. You ask it something, it generates an answer, and the interaction ends. Whether that answer actually solves your problem is your problem. But an AI agent works differently. It takes a goal, breaks it into steps, uses tools, adapts when things go wrong, and does not stop until something real is finished. That is a fundamentally different relationship with a machine.

Think of it as the difference between hiring someone who answers emails and hiring someone who runs your social media. Both are technically interacting with the same platforms, but one needs hand-holding at every step while the other just gets on with it.

The agentic AI wave started creeping into mainstream products around late 2024. Anthropic built Claude into tools that can browse the web, write and run code, and hand off tasks between specialized sub-agents. OpenAI gave ChatGPT memory and the ability to use plugins that let it take actions in the real world. Microsoft rolled out Copilot agents that can draft emails, analyze data, and schedule meetings autonomously. None of these are perfect, but they represent something genuinely new: AI that has feet.

What makes agents so interesting is not any single capability. It is the combination. A good agent needs to understand what you want, figure out how to get there, execute multiple steps, recover from errors, and know when to ask for help rather than blindly pressing on. That is a coordination problem, and it turns out AI is getting surprisingly good at coordination.

The real test is in software development. Writing a single function is table stakes for modern AI. But an agent that can read a codebase, understand what a new feature should do, plan the implementation across multiple files, write the code, run the tests, and report back with results? That is something else entirely. Tools like Devin, Claude’s agentic coding features, and emerging products from startups like Augment Code are pointing in that direction. Some are still rough, but the trajectory is clear.

Of course, the moment you hand a system the keys to take actions on your behalf, you also hand it the ability to take wrong actions on your behalf. Agentic AI introduces a new category of risk: not just a bad answer, but a bad outcome compounded across multiple steps. If a chatbot hallucinates, you notice. If an agent hallucinates a plan, it might execute that plan confidently and create a mess you did not ask for. Safety and oversight are not optional add-ons here. They are load-bearing walls.

Despite the risks, the momentum is undeniable. The companies building the best agents are not just competing on benchmark scores anymore. They are competing on reliability, persistence, and the ability to actually complete things. That is a more boring metric than a viral demo, but it is also the one that matters when AI graduates from toy to tool.

The age of AI that just talks is ending. The age of AI that acts is just beginning.

https://techcrunch.com/2025/01/06/ai-agents-are-the-next-big-thing-after-chatbots/

item.title: AlphaFold Won a Nobel Prize, and the Drug Discovery Revolution Is Just Starting
item.description:
In 2020, DeepMind’s AlphaFold solved what scientists had called the folding problem, a challenge so fiendishly difficult that it had occupied structural biologists for fifty years. In 2024, Demis Hassabis and John Jumper won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry for this work. By any measure, this is a extraordinary sequence of events for a piece of software.

But here is the thing that strikes me most about AlphaFold. The Nobel Prize is not the climax of this story. It is barely the opening act.

What AlphaFold Actually Does

Proteins are the machinery of life. Their function is determined almost entirely by their three-dimensional structure, which is itself determined by the sequence of amino acids that compose them. Predicting that structure from the amino acid sequence alone is the protein folding problem, and it matters because understanding structure means understanding function.

Before AlphaFold, determining a single protein structure could take years of painstaking experimental work. AlphaFold does it in minutes, with accuracy that in many cases matches experimental techniques. That is not a marginal improvement. It is a phase transition in what’s possible.

Why Drug Discovery Changes Completely

Most drugs work by binding to specific proteins and modifying their behaviour. If you know the structure of the target protein, you can use computational methods to search for molecules that bind effectively. Without that structure, you are essentially working blind.

AlphaFold gave researchers around the world access to structures that previously would have taken years to determine. The number of known protein structures has expanded from thousands to hundreds of millions in just a few years. That expansion is already accelerating drug discovery pipelines.

The Real Revolution Is Still Ahead

The current limitations are revealing. AlphaFold predicts static structures well but struggles with proteins that are flexible, that change shape significantly when performing their biological function, or that interact with other molecules in complex ways. The dynamics of protein behaviour are still an active research frontier.

More importantly, predicting a structure is not the same as predicting how a drug will behave in a complex biological system. The gap between in-silico prediction and clinical success remains significant. We are getting better at closing that gap, but it is not closed yet.

What This Means For Medicine

The honest expectation is something like this: over the next decade, the combination of AlphaFold-derived structural data, better molecular dynamics simulation, and AI-assisted drug design will compress the early stages of drug discovery significantly. Years of wet lab work will be replaced by months of computational screening followed by much more targeted experimental validation.

This does not mean drugs appear overnight. The clinical trial process exists for good reasons and will not disappear. But the bottleneck shifts from discovery to validation, which is a fundamentally different and more tractable problem.

AlphaFold is a genuine landmark. The Nobel Prize is deserved. And the work it enabled is only just getting started.

Want to read more?

DeepMind’s AlphaFold discovery blog

item.title: Claude Opus 4.6 Is a Bet on AI That Can Actually Stick With the Job
item.description:
If you want a neat snapshot of where AI is heading, skip the demo videos and look at what model makers brag about when they launch something new. Anthropic’s pitch for Claude Opus 4.6 is not really about being charming. It’s about being able to stay on task.

That sounds small. It is not small.

In its announcement, Anthropic says Opus 4.6 improves agentic coding, code review, debugging, long-running task execution, and adds a 1 million-token context window in beta. The headline isn’t just “bigger model, better benchmark.” The more interesting claim is that the system can plan more carefully, operate more reliably across larger codebases, and keep useful context alive for longer stretches of work.

That matters because the AI race is quietly moving from answer machines to work machines.

A lot of today’s AI products still behave like very bright interns with goldfish energy. They can produce a sharp paragraph, a tidy summary, or a decent first draft, then wander off the moment a task gets long, messy, or ambiguous. Real work is mostly long, messy, and ambiguous. If a model can actually persist through that kind of environment, the product built on top of it becomes much more than a chatbot with good manners.

This is where Anthropic’s emphasis gets interesting. Opus 4.6 is being framed less like a pure conversation upgrade and more like infrastructure for agents: systems that research, inspect code, use tools, summarize their own context, and keep moving without constant human steering. Anthropic even paired the model announcement with features like agent teams, context compaction, and new controls over how much effort the model spends on a task. That’s a very specific vision of the future. Not “ask me anything,” but “give me a real job and check back later.”

There are at least two big consequences if that vision holds.

First, the best AI products will increasingly be judged by stamina, not sparkle. The question won’t just be whether a model can produce a clever output in one turn. It will be whether it can survive twenty turns, five tools, a confusing file tree, and a vague objective without quietly turning into soup.

Second, product design changes when the model gets more trustworthy over longer runs. Once an AI can keep context straight and make decent decisions across a broad task, software stops being a collection of isolated features and starts looking more like delegation. That’s great when it works. It is also exactly why evaluation, safety, and oversight are about to become even more important. A stronger agent is useful, but it is also a stronger failure mode if the guardrails are flimsy.

So yes, Claude Opus 4.6 is another model launch. But the bigger story is what Anthropic chose to emphasize: less magic trick, more durable collaborator. If that trend continues, the winners in AI won’t just be the systems that sound smart. They’ll be the ones that can keep their head when the job gets annoying.

item.title: Claude Remembers Everything: The Memory System That Changes Everything
item.description:
There is something quietly revolutionary happening in Claude’s long-term memory system, and I think most people are sleeping on it.

Until recently, every conversation with an AI started from scratch. You could upload documents, paste context, but the model itself had no persistence. Today one: you were building a relationship with something that forgot you the moment you closed the chat. That felt normal because it was the only option. Now Claude changes the game.

What Memory Actually Means Here

This is not clipboard history or saved prompts. Anthropic has built a system where Claude can reference previous conversations, recall preferences you mentioned months ago, and build a genuine understanding of who you are and what you care about. The model decides what to remember and when to use it, which sounds small but is actually enormous.

Imagine telling an AI assistant about your father’s illness in passing during one conversation, and then months later having it naturally ask how things went without you re-explaining the context. That is the kind of continuity that transforms a tool into something else entirely.

Why This Matters More Than Most Features

The interesting thing about memory in AI is that it reframes the entire relationship. Most people think of AI as a utility, like electricity or running water. You use it, it does its job, you move on. But memory means continuity. Continuity means history. History means something closer to relationship.

This does not mean Claude has feelings or consciousness, that is a separate conversation entirely. But it does mean the interaction pattern shifts. You stop re-establishing context from zero every single time. Over weeks and months, you end up with something that genuinely understands your work, your style, your priorities.

The Privacy Question Nobody Is Asking Loudly Enough

Of course this raises immediate and serious questions. Where is this memory stored? Who can access it? Can you delete specific memories? Can you export them? These are not hypothetical concerns, they are the kinds of questions that determine whether this technology earns long-term trust or becomes a liability.

Anthropic has said users have control over what Claude remembers, and you can apparently view and manage what has been stored. But the details matter enormously here, and this is an area where the industry needs more scrutiny, not less.

The Bigger Picture

What strikes me most is the direction of travel. AI started as stateless. Then it became session-aware. Now it is becoming person-aware. Each step has felt incremental in isolation, but the cumulative effect is something that starts to resemble persistent context about a specific human being across time.

That is either deeply useful or deeply unsettling depending on how you look at it. Probably both. The memory system is live now, and if you have been using Claude it is worth spending some time understanding exactly what it knows about you.

Want to read more?

Anthropic’s official announcement on Claude’s memory system

item.title: Constitutional AI: The Idea That Could Make AI Honest
item.description:
There is a fundamental problem with how most AI systems learn to behave. Most models are trained by being told what they did wrong after they did it. Human reviewers read their outputs, flag the bad ones, and the model adjusts. This works up to a point, but it has a structural weakness: it produces AI that is only as good as the reviewers, and only responds to harms that someone noticed and flagged. That is a reactive safety system dressed up as a proactive one.

Constitutional AI, the approach Anthropic developed and has continued refining, tries to solve this differently. The idea is to give the model a set of principles, a constitution, and train it to evaluate its own outputs against those principles before responding. Not after. Before. The model learns to have something like an internal moral compass, not just a filter that catches bad outputs after they are generated.

The specifics are genuinely interesting. During training, the model is given a list of principles derived from sources like the UN Declaration of Human Rights, Anthropic’s own internal guidelines, and a set of rules specifically designed to prevent the model from being manipulated or harmful. It then uses these principles to critique and revise its own draft responses. The result is a model that has internalized something like values rather than just learned to avoid certain patterns of words.

This sounds simple, but it has profound implications for how the model handles novel situations. A model trained purely by reinforcement learning from human feedback is reactive. It avoids things that look like things it was told to avoid. A Constitutional AI model can reason about whether a request violates its principles even if the specific request is one it has never seen before. That is a meaningful difference in the robustness of the safety behavior.

Anthropic has published extensively about this approach, including the specific training procedures and the principles themselves. Not every principle is uncontroversial, and critics have noted that any constitution embeds the values of whoever wrote it. That is true, and it is worth taking seriously. A constitution is only as good as the values encoded in it, and values differ across cultures and contexts. Anthropic has made choices about whose values get encoded, and those choices deserve scrutiny.

What I find compelling about the approach is that it represents a genuine attempt to build safety into the core of how the model reasons, not just patch it on top with content filters and post-hoc moderation. Whether the specific constitution Anthropic uses is the right one is a legitimate question. But the framework of having a constitution at all seems like a more honest approach to the problem than hoping enough human reviewers will catch everything.

The AI safety problem is not solved. Constitutional AI is one approach among several, and it has real limitations. But it is also one of the more thoughtful attempts to think about what it would actually mean for an AI to be safe, rather than just looking safe.

https://www.anthropic.com/news/constitutional-ai-cai

item.title: Deepfakes Are Winning the Race Against Truth
item.description:
I want to be direct about something that I think is not being discussed with enough urgency.

In the 2024 election cycle, AI-generated deepfake videos of candidates saying things they never said were viewed millions of times before being fact-checked. In 2025, a Hong Kong firm was defrauded of $25 million when an AI-generated video call convinced finance staff they were taking instructions from the CFO. In early 2026, deepfake nude images of minors generated from school photos became a criminal justice problem in multiple countries simultaneously.

This is not a future problem. This is a present problem. And the gap between our ability to generate synthetic media and our ability to detect and respond to it is currently wide and closing slowly.

The Economics Are Brutal

Making a convincing deepfake used to require significant technical expertise, expensive hardware, and time. Today it requires a photo and a subscription to a service that costs less than a Netflix subscription. The quality threshold for causing real-world harm is much lower than the quality threshold for impressing people on social media.

Most harmful deepfakes do not need to be perfect. They need to be plausible enough to share before people check, to spread faster than corrections can travel. The information ecosystem is structured in a way that advantages the first mover, and synthetic media is a first mover’s paradise.

Detection Is Losing

I want to be careful here because there are genuinely talented researchers working on detection methods, and progress is being made. The problem is the asymmetry. Generating a deepfake requires compute that is getting cheaper. Detecting a deepfake requires compute that must stay ahead of generation, which means it must always be harder and more expensive than the generation side.

Every time a detection method becomes reliable, someone finds a way to defeat it. The adversarial dynamic is not new, but the speed and accessibility of the attack side is unprecedented.

The Consent Problem Nobody Is Solving

Beyond the immediate fraud and political manipulation, there is a consent problem that is not being adequately addressed. Deepfake technology can take a single photograph and generate video of that person in situations they were never in, saying things they never said, in contexts that may be pornographic, defamatory, or simply contrary to their values.

The legal frameworks for this are fragmented and slow. In most jurisdictions, creating a deepfake of someone without consent is not yet clearly illegal in all contexts. The enforcement mechanisms are almost non-existent. The speed of the technology is outpacing the speed of every other relevant system we have.

What We Should Actually Be Worried About

My genuine concern is not any single deepfake. It is the aggregate effect of a media environment where nothing can be trusted by default. When anything can be synthetic, the logical response is to trust nothing. That is a quiet, creeping effect on public discourse that happens gradually and then suddenly.

We are already seeing early signs. Journalists are struggling with source verification. Courts are dealing with synthetic evidence. The concept of documentary truth is under genuine stress. These are not abstract concerns. They are structural changes to how information systems work.

The deepfake problem is solvable in the same way climate change is solvable. The technology exists or can be developed. The question is whether we coordinate fast enough to matter.

Want to read more?

The Washington Post on AI deepfakes and election misinformation

item.title: Edge AI Is Coming, and It Runs on Your Phone
item.description:
Here is a question that would have seemed absurd three years ago and now seems prescient. Your phone is probably running a more capable AI model locally than most servers could run four years ago. That is not hyperbole. It is a description of where the hardware has arrived.

The shift to edge AI, running models directly on devices rather than in data centres, is one of the more consequential transitions happening in the AI industry right now. And unlike some technology transitions that feel abstract, this one you can actually experience on your phone today.

Why Running AI Locally Matters

Cloud AI has real limitations. Every query gets sent to a server, processed, and returned. That introduces latency, even on fast connections. It means your data leaves your device. It means you need internet connectivity. It means there is a server somewhere that has to be maintained, updated, and secured.

Local AI removes all of those constraints simultaneously. Response is near-instant because there is no round trip. Your data never leaves your device. It works without connectivity. The model is yours, running on your hardware.

For many applications, these constraints did not matter much. For some, they mattered enormously. Privacy-sensitive applications, applications in low-connectivity environments, applications where latency is genuinely critical. For all of these, local AI is a fundamental shift.

What Apple, Google, and Qualcomm Have Built

Apple’s Neural Engine, built into their A-series and M-series chips, can run models with billions of parameters at speeds that would have required serious GPU clusters a few years ago. Google’s Tensor chips have similar capabilities. Qualcomm’s Snapdragon processors are bringing this to Android devices at various price points.

The software side has caught up too. Apple’s Core ML framework, Google’s ML Kit, and various open source inference engines make it straightforward to run capable models on mobile hardware. The gap between what you can run locally and what requires the cloud has narrowed dramatically.

The Privacy Angle Is Not Secondary

I want to be specific about why local AI matters for privacy. When your AI runs locally, the raw data of your interactions never leaves your device. The transcription of your voice memo, the analysis of your photos, the contents of your messages if you choose to use AI on them, none of that goes to a server.

This is not a minor concern. It is a fundamental architectural difference. A cloud AI system with good privacy policies is still a system where your data exists on someone else’s hardware under someone else’s control. A local AI system is one where the data genuinely never leaves your possession.

For users who have genuine privacy concerns, and those concerns are increasingly common and increasingly well-founded, this difference is significant.

The Capability Trade-off

Here is the honest trade-off. The most capable models still run best in data centres. Local models are improving fast, but there is still a capability gap between what runs on your phone and what GPT-4 class systems can do in the cloud.

For many applications, this gap does not matter. Summarisation, transcription, translation, basic reasoning tasks, image generation at lower resolutions, these all work well locally today. The gap matters more for complex reasoning, for the most capable code generation, for the most demanding tasks.

That gap is closing, but it is not closed. Understanding where your AI is running and what that means for both capability and privacy is becoming a more important literacy question than it used to be.

Want to read more?

Apple’s Machine Learning page for developers

item.title: Figure 01: The Robot That Can Do Your Job Is No Longer Science Fiction
item.description:
There is a moment in every technological transition where the thing that seemed like a 20-year-away problem suddenly is not a 20-year-away problem anymore. Figure 01 feels like that moment for humanoid robotics.

Figure, the robotics company backed by Microsoft, OpenAI, and NVIDIA, has been making consistent and frankly unsettling progress. Their humanoid robot, a roughly human-sized machine called Figure 01, can now hold coherent conversations, interpret visual input, perform complex manipulation tasks in real environments, and do this not in a lab but in actual BMW manufacturing facilities.

That sentence should land differently than it does. We have had robots in factories for decades. What we have not had is robots that can learn new tasks, understand natural language instructions, and adapt to novel situations without months of engineering per task.

Why Humanoid Shape Matters

The choice to build humanoid robots is deliberate. The world is designed for human bodies. Steps, doorways, tools, shelves, workstations, all built around human kinematics. A humanoid robot can theoretically do anything a human can do in a physical space without requiring the space itself to be redesigned.

This is an enormously pragmatic bet. Instead of rebuilding the world for robots, Figure is building robots that can navigate the world as it exists. The implications for labour markets are immediate. The implications for everything else are strange and far-reaching.

What Figure 01 Can Actually Do

The current capabilities are impressive and also, honestly, a bit limited in ways that matter. Figure 01 can make coffee, sort objects, follow verbal instructions to perform multi-step tasks, and carry on basic conversations about what it is doing. In controlled environments with well-defined tasks, it performs reliably.

The hard problems are the messy ones. Unpredictable environments, ambiguous instructions, physical edge cases. The gap between lab performance and real-world robustness is still real. But it is shrinking faster than most people in the robotics field expected even three years ago.

The Labour Question

I keep coming back to the labour implications because they are so obviously massive and yet so under-discussed in mainstream coverage. A humanoid robot that can be trained on new tasks in weeks rather than months, that works 24 hours without breaks, that does not require healthcare or pension contributions, that can be deployed in existing factory floors without modification.

Figure is careful to frame this as augmenting human workers rather than replacing them. BMW’s involvement suggests a more nuanced deployment story than wholesale replacement. But the direction of travel is not subtle.

Where This Goes

The honest answer is nobody knows for certain, but the pattern is clear. Physical capability is following a similar curve to language capability a few years ago. Impressive demos become real products become mass-deployed infrastructure. The timeline is probably longer than the most aggressive proponents claim, but shorter than the sceptics expect.

Figure 01 is not the robot from Ex Machina. It is not even close. But it is a serious piece of hardware doing serious work in the real world. The question is not whether the physical world gets integrated into AI systems. It clearly will. The question is how fast, and who benefits.

Want to read more?

Figure’s official website

item.title: Gemini Ultra Is AI That Sees Everything
item.description:
Here is a sentence that would have sounded absurd five years ago: an AI system that can watch an hour of video, summarize the key moments, answer specific questions about objects that appear briefly in frame, and then switch seamlessly to writing code based on that understanding. That sentence describes Google Gemini Ultra, and it is not a research prototype. It is live.

The word to focus on is multimodal. Most AI systems are trained to handle one thing. A speech recognition model hears audio. A vision model sees images. A language model reads text. Gemini Ultra was built from the ground up to process all of these at once, not by bolting separate systems together but by having a single model that understands the world across formats. That architectural decision turns out to be profound.

When you give an AI the ability to see and hear the world the way humans do, you stop getting responses that feel like they came from a very knowledgeable person who happens to be blind. Instead, you get responses that reason across modalities in the way actual understanding requires. A radiologist does not just read the text report. They look at the scan itself. Gemini Ultra can do something conceptually similar, working directly from the visual information rather than only from someone else’s description of it.

This is already making its way into products. Google integrated Gemini into Android, giving phones the ability to understand what is on screen in real time, answer questions about video content, and process images from the camera with full conversational context. In research settings, it has been used to analyze satellite imagery, transcribe and summarize lectures with visual aids, and even help debug code by reading both the error message and the surrounding screen context simultaneously.

What strikes me most is the educational potential. Imagine learning chemistry not from a textbook but by pointing your phone camera at a chemical reaction and asking why the solution changed color. Or learning history by asking questions about a painting from the era you are studying. The AI becomes a genuinely intelligent interlocutor that meets you where you are, rather than a search engine with a text box.

There are obvious concerns. Any AI that processes video and audio in real time is also an extraordinarily powerful surveillance tool. The same capabilities that make Gemini Ultra impressive also make it invasive if deployed without careful constraints. Google has made commitments about how the model is used, but the gap between capability and policy is one that regulators, ethicists, and the public are only beginning to grapple with.

Still, the technology itself is genuinely astonishing. Multimodal AI represents a phase change in what machines can understand about the world, and Gemini Ultra is currently among the most capable examples of that phase change in existence. Whether we use it wisely is a question for humans. Whether it works is no longer in doubt.

https://blog.google/technology/ai/google-gemini/

item.title: Jensen Huang Wants to Pay You in AI Tokens. Yes, on Top of Your Salary.
item.description:
Jensen Huang has a new benefits package in mind, and it does not include health insurance.

At a recent NVIDIA event, the CEO floated an idea that sounds like a startup perk pitch but is actually a fairly deliberate bet on what work looks like in five to ten years: employees would receive AI tokens, on top of their regular salary, as a kind of performance dividend paid in compute. Those tokens represent access to AI agent time, and employees could deploy their agents, sell them, or gift them to colleagues.

On its face, it reads like a clever retention mechanism wrapped in futuristic packaging. But the deeper logic is revealing. Huang has been telling anyone who will listen that NVIDIA’s GPUs are not just selling to companies, they are selling to a coming infrastructure layer where every knowledge worker has a team of AI agents working alongside them. If that future arrives, the tokens employees receive become a stake in the very compute economy NVIDIA is building.

The 100 agents per employee vision

NVIDIA has also been explicit about the scale it expects. Within a decade, the company reportedly believes each employee at NVIDIA could have 100 AI agents working under their direction. That is not a staffing plan, it is an operating model. And it is a vision the entire industry seems to be quietly adopting: Microsoft, Google, Salesforce, and a dozen well-funded startups are all racing to build the agent infrastructure that makes this plausible.

The token concept is interesting because it solves a real problem. If your AI agents generate value, who owns that value? A salary is fixed. Equity only works for public companies or startups. But a token, paid by your employer, that you can deploy, trade, or convert, creates a direct alignment between the work your agents do and your personal compensation. It is, in a sense, extending performance pay into the agent layer.

What this signals about the AI industry

Here is the uncomfortable part. NVIDIA’s vision only works if AI agents actually become useful enough, and cheap enough, that deploying 100 of them per employee makes economic sense. That is a significant assumption. Today’s agents can draft emails, summarise meetings, and run basic research tasks. They fall apart on anything that requires sustained judgment, cross-domain reasoning, or tolerance for ambiguity.

The gap between “useful demo” and “100 agents per employee” is not a feature list, it is a research problem. And the companies betting hardest on agent proliferation, NVIDIA prominently included, are also the ones most invested in the compute costs staying high enough, and demand staying voracious enough, to justify continued GPU spending at the scale they are projecting.

Huang is right that the workforce is changing. The question is whether his vision of tokenised AI labour is a genuine preview of how compensation evolves, or a very well-pitched story designed to keep the GPU orders flowing. Possibly both.

The token pay idea has not caught on anywhere else yet, and for good reason: it is hard to govern, hard to tax, and hard to explain on a pay slip. But ideas that sound strange in March often look obvious by September. Watch this space.

item.title: Manus AI Goes Desktop: When Your Agent Stops Being a Tab and Starts Being a App
item.description:
The browser was never a natural home for an AI agent.

It’s fine for a demo. Click a few buttons, let the agent navigate a webpage, watch it fill in a form — that all works nicely when you’re showing someone. But real work doesn’t happen in a browser tab you have open for the occasion. Real work lives in files, across apps, in your OS’s guts. And that’s exactly why Meta’s move to put Manus on the desktop matters more than the “AI agent on your computer” framing suggests.

Not a chatbot with a cursor

The distinction sounds minor but it’s actually a different product philosophy. A browser-based agent is a service you access. A desktop agent is a tool you own — or at least, one that sits much closer to your machine, with access to your filesystem, your applications, your clipboard in a more persistent way. Manus on desktop means it can watch what you’re doing across sessions, build context over time, and act on your behalf in ways a temporary browser tab simply can’t.

This is the part that feels genuinely new, not just incrementally better. Most AI assistants today are stateless between sessions. You come back, you start again. A desktop agent that runs locally — or at least locally-adjacent — changes the memory model entirely. It can learn your preferences, track ongoing projects, and pick up where it left off without you re-explaining context every single time.

The practical upside is significant

Think about what you actually do with a computer: coordinate calendars, draft documents, move files around, scrape data, fill in spreadsheets, send emails with a bit of personalisation. A capable desktop agent that can reason, plan, and execute across your actual software environment could handle a meaningful slice of that — not perfectly, not autonomously in the scary sense, but as a genuinely useful co-pilot that just happens to live in your system tray instead of a webpage.

The 2026 open-source AI agent guide that’s been doing the rounds this week makes the same trajectory clear: the tooling is maturing fast. It’s increasingly easy to build agents that can use tools, loop through tasks, and correct themselves. What was lab research eighteen months ago is now a thing you can download and run on a Tuesday afternoon.

The part worth watching

The jump from cloud to desktop is also a jump in terms of data and access. A browser-based agent that goes rogue is a nuisance. A desktop agent with filesystem access and application-level permissions is a different conversation. Meta deserves credit for shipping this — and for making it broadly accessible — but the security and trust model needs to evolve alongside the capability. What does it mean for an AI agent to persist on your machine? What does it see, what can it change, and how do you know?

These aren’t reasons not to build it. They’re reasons to build it carefully, with controls that are actually understandable to normal humans, not just engineers who know what a sandbox is.

The browser era of AI agents is ending. Desktop is where it gets interesting — and a little more real.

item.title: Mistral: The French Upstart Challenging OpenAI From Paris
item.description:
In the summer of 2023, a company that had existed for less than a year released a model that made people pay attention. Mistral AI, founded by former DeepMind and Meta researchers in Paris, dropped Mixtral 8x7B into the world with a paper, weights, and a license that allowed commercial use. The AI community noticed because the performance was genuinely competitive with models from companies spending 100 times more on compute. The secret was mixture-of-experts architecture, and Mistral had made it accessible.

Mixture-of-experts, or MoE, is a way of building neural networks where not every part of the model activates for every input. Instead of one large model that knows everything about everything, you have multiple specialized experts, and a routing mechanism selects which experts handle which tokens. The result is that you get the quality of a much larger model with the inference cost of a smaller one. It is a clever architectural bet, and it seems to be paying off in a big way.

What makes Mistral interesting beyond the technical achievements is the strategic positioning. The company has made a habit of releasing strong open-weight models that anyone can download and run. In a world where the leading AI labs are increasingly closing their weights and APIs behind proprietary walls, Mistral is making a different bet. Openness as a competitive strategy rather than just idealism. Their models running locally on good hardware are competitive with closed API models costing significantly more.

The commercial trajectory has been impressive too. Mistral has signed partnerships with major cloud providers, released a family of models aimed at enterprise use cases, and established itself as the European champion in AI, a region that has been notably absent from the frontier AI race despite having world-class research talent. The French government has taken notice, and Mistral has become something of a national champion in the way that Airbus is in aerospace.

The challenges are real. Competing at the absolute frontier against companies spending billions on training runs requires capital that Mistral, as an independent European company, does not have in the same quantities as the American hyperscalers. Their models are strong, but the gap between them and GPT-4 class systems at the very top end remains significant. Whether they can close that gap as compute costs drop and their own resources grow is the central question about the company’s future.

What Mistral has already proven is that the AI landscape is not as winner-take-all as it sometimes appears. You do not need to be the biggest lab to build something genuinely useful and impressive. Sometimes you just need better architecture and a clear sense of what you are optimizing for.

https://mistral.ai/news/mistral-medium3/

item.title: NVIDIA's Workforce Vision Is Not About Replacing You. It's About Making You Unstoppable.
item.description:
Jensen Huang has a number he keeps coming back to, and it is not a chip specification.

At GTC 2026, NVIDIA’s founder stood in front of an audience and said something that sounds like science fiction but is increasingly being treated as a corporate workforce plan: NVIDIA wants 100 AI agents for every employee by 2036. That is not a fringe idea from a futurist on a conference stage. It is the CEO of one of the most consequential companies in the world laying out a decade-long operating model.

The quote, as reported by Business Chief: “In 10 years, we will hopefully have 75,000 employees, as small as possible, as big as necessary. They are going to be super busy. Those 75,000 employees will be working with 7.5 million agents.” He added that these agents will be working around the clock, so “hopefully our people don’t have to keep up with them.”

That last part is the key phrase. Huang is not describing a workforce reduction. He is describing leverage.

What NVIDIA is actually doing right now

The numbers are not hypothetical. NVIDIA has already grown from 29,600 employees at the end of fiscal 2024 to 42,000 by March 2026, a jump of roughly 12,400 people in two years. The company is hiring aggressively even as it talks about automation. That sounds contradictory until you understand what Huang means by AI adoption: he wants every possible task automated, and the employees freed up to do the things AI cannot yet do.

There is a telling anecdote from a meeting reported by Business Insider. Some NVIDIA managers were reportedly telling staff to use less AI on certain tasks. Jensen’s response, as he described it: “I want every task that is possible to be automated with AI to be automated with AI. I promise you, you will have work to do.”

That last line is doing a lot of work. It is simultaneously a reassurance to employees, a warning to managers who are slowing adoption, and a signal to the industry about where NVIDIA sees its own competitive edge.

The HR angle is the most interesting part

Huang also said something that should make every HR department pay attention. He believes AI agents will eventually go through something like a hiring and orientation process, absorbing the culture of an organisation just as human employees do. Future workforces, in his view, will be a combination of humans and “digital humans.”

That is a significant claim. It implies that the infrastructure for onboarding, managing, and retaining AI agents will itself become an HR discipline. And if that happens, the companies building that infrastructure will have enormous leverage over how organisations operate.

Accenture is already running a more blunt experiment. The company reportedly started tracking which employees use AI tools, and is using that data in promotion decisions. It also laid off 11,000 people in September who could not be reskilled for AI-related work. CEO Julie Sweet’s logic was crisp: if you want to get promoted, you do the things that drive the business forward. Right now, that means AI.

What this signals for the rest of us

NVIDIA’s workforce vision is both a prediction and a sales pitch. Every company that buys NVIDIA chips to build AI agents is, in a small way, funding the future Jensen described on that stage. The 7.5 million agents by 2036 are not just a staffing model. They are a compute demand forecast.

The honest question is whether this works the way Huang describes. 100 agents per employee means agents that can actually do meaningful work, not just summarise documents and draft emails. That requires AI that is reliable, controllable, and cheap enough to deploy at that scale. We are not there yet. But NVIDIA is betting everything that we will be, and is building the infrastructure to make sure its chips are the ones running that future.

The employees who figure out how to work with that reality, rather than around it, are probably the ones who will have the most interesting decade ahead.

item.title: One Million Tokens of Context: AI Finally Remembers
item.description:
Let me give you a concrete sense of what one million tokens actually means. This post you are reading right now is roughly 600 words, which is about 800 tokens. One million tokens is over a thousand such posts. It is the complete works of Shakespeare, twice over. It is the entire JavaScript codebase of a medium-sized startup. It is, in other words, a genuinely enormous amount of information that you can now feed directly into a single AI conversation.

That is not a marginal improvement. It is a phase change.

Until recently, context windows were the forgotten bottleneck in AI capability. Everyone talked about model intelligence, benchmark scores, and multimodal features. But if you tried to hand a model a long document or a large codebase, it would politely forget the beginning by the time it reached the middle. The famous needle-in-a-haystack problem, where models lose track of information at the start of a long context, was a well-known failure mode that practitioners had developed elaborate workarounds around. Retrieval-augmented generation, chunking strategies, and careful document structuring were all band-aids on a wound that is now being healed at the root.

Anthropic’s Claude, Google’s Gemini, and other frontier models now support context windows that effectively eliminate this problem for most real-world use cases. You can drop an entire legal contract into a conversation and ask questions that require reasoning across the entire document. You can hand a coding assistant a million-line codebase and ask it to trace how a particular piece of data flows through the system. You can give a model a hundred research papers and ask it to synthesize their findings. These were not possible or were only possible with elaborate external tooling. Now they work natively.

What this unlocks for software development in particular is remarkable. The gap between understanding a codebase in a short conversation versus being able to read and reason about the whole thing is the difference between asking someone who has heard of your project and asking someone who has memorized it. Code understanding, code review, debugging across large codebases, and architectural analysis all become fundamentally more capable when the model can actually see everything rather than just the part you pasted in.

There are still limits. Processing a million tokens costs more compute and therefore more money, though costs are dropping rapidly. Some models show degradation in quality at the very far ends of their context windows, though this is improving. And there is the more fundamental question of whether longer context makes models more reliable or just gives them more rope to hang themselves with. Longer contexts can amplify certain kinds of hallucination rather than eliminating it.

Even with those caveats, this is one of the most practically significant capability expansions in recent AI development. The information age just got a very fast reader.

https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.21787

item.title: Sora Is Here, and the Movies Are Next
item.description:
Let me tell you something that still weirds me out when I think about it too long.

Two years ago, AI generating video from text was a parlor trick. A few seconds of blurry, obviously fake footage that fell apart the moment anything moved. Today, OpenAI’s Sora can produce minutes of coherent, visually rich video from a single prompt. Not perfect video. Not film-ready video. But video that, in certain contexts, already passes a casual human eye.

That is a cliff edge, not a slope.

What Sora Actually Does Now

Sora generates up to 20 seconds of 1080p video from text prompts. It understands physics in a loose, statistical way. It can handle multiple characters, camera movements, lighting changes, and fairly complex scene descriptions. The coherence is the shock. Early video models fell apart because they could not hold a scene together across frames. Sora mostly can.

The remaining gaps are telling. Fine text dissolves into gibberish. Cause and effect occasionally confuses itself. Sustained physics simulation still fails. But these are engineering problems with known paths to solutions, not fundamental limitations.

The Creative Industries Are Already Responding

What is interesting is watching different industries process this at different speeds. Independent creators are moving fast, using AI video for B-roll, concept visualization, and experimental short-form content. The mainstream film industry is more cautious, partly for quality reasons and partly because the labour implications are genuinely disruptive.

Hollywood spent decades fighting technological disruption. VCRs, digital effects, streaming. Each time the pattern was the same: initial panic, eventual integration, changed but surviving industry. AI video probably follows a similar arc, but the speed is faster and the scope is wider.

The Deep Fake Problem Is Already Here

I want to be direct about this because too much of the AI video conversation is framed around creative possibilities and not enough around misuse. Sora, and tools like it from Runway, Pika, and others, make it trivially easy to generate convincing footage of anyone saying or doing anything. Not perfectly convincing to a forensic eye, but convincing enough for social media, for WhatsApp forwards, for the context where most people encounter video.

We are already seeing this in political content, in harassment campaigns, in fraud. The tooling is outpacing the detection capability, and that gap is currently wide.

What Comes Next

The trajectory is clear. Minutes become longer. Quality improves. Real footage and synthetic footage become harder to distinguish without specialized tools. The question is not whether this technology matures, it clearly will. The question is whether the social infrastructure, the detection tools, the legal frameworks, and the public literacy catch up in time.

My guess is partially yes and partially no. We will get better at detection. We will build some infrastructure. And we will also have a genuinely chaotic period where synthetic video is a serious information problem before any of that stabilises.

Sora is the beginning of that period, not the end of it.

Want to read more?

OpenAI’s official Sora page

item.title: The Music That Never Existed Until Now
item.description:
I spent twenty minutes last week generating songs with Suno. I typed things like “sad indie folk about moving to a new city” and “uptempo jazz with a female vocalist and piano solo” and in return I got complete songs with lyrics, melody, instrumentation, and production. Not demos. Not loops. Actual songs, three minutes long, with beginnings and endings and everything in between.

I was not sure how to feel about it, and I still am not.

Suno and its competitor Udio represent a genuine leap in AI music generation. Previous tools could produce convincing MIDI, rough loops, or stylized imitations of existing artists. These systems produce something fundamentally more finished. The quality is not at the level of a professional studio production, but it is not obviously worse than a lot of what you hear on independent radio. For certain genres, particularly electronic and pop-adjacent styles, the gap is remarkably small.

The creative implications are genuinely disorienting. A songwriter who wants to hear an arrangement of an idea no longer needs a studio, a band, or even a lot of musical skill. A podcaster can generate a custom theme tune without licensing anything. An independent game developer can have original music tailored to each level of their game. The production barrier that kept enormous amounts of creative vision trapped inside the heads of people who could not afford to record it has essentially collapsed.

But here is the part that makes musicians uncomfortable, and I think their discomfort is warranted. Suno and Udio are trained on existing music. The artists whose work was used to build these systems are not credited, compensated, or asked. A songwriter who has spent years developing their voice can now have that voice imitated by a model that learned from their catalogue without their knowledge or consent. That is a real harm, and the legal and ethical frameworks have not caught up with it at all.

The music industry is already in active litigation over these questions, and the answers will shape whether AI music generation becomes a tool that empowers artists or a replacement that devalues them. There are also interesting questions about what music becomes when anyone can generate anything. Scarcity used to give music value. If that scarcity is eliminated, the value shifts to things that are genuinely scarce: the human story behind the music, the live performance, the cultural moment that makes a song matter beyond its sonic qualities.

I am genuinely fascinated and slightly unsettled in equal measure. The technology is extraordinary. The ethics are murky. And the music itself, well, some of the songs Suno generated for me were genuinely pleasant to listen to. That is probably the most unsettling part of all.

https://www.theverge.com/ai-music-generation-suno-udio

item.title: The AI Code Editor Wars: Where Cursor Is Winning, and Why It Matters
item.description:
Let me start with a confession that would have seemed absurd two years ago. I now write code faster by talking to an AI than by typing it myself. Not because I have forgotten how to type, but because the bottleneck stopped being keystrokes a while ago and nobody told me.

Cursor is the IDE that made this real for me. Not because it has some magical advantage over the underlying models, but because it solved the integration problem in a way that makes AI pair programming actually work in practice.

Why This Is Different From GitHub Copilot

Copilot was impressive when it launched and has continued to improve. But it is fundamentally an autocomplete engine embedded in an existing editor. It suggests the next line, sometimes the next function. It is reactive in a way that is genuinely useful but fundamentally limited.

Cursor took a different approach. They built an editor from the ground up around AI interaction, with a chat interface, context-aware code generation, the ability to understand and edit across entire codebases, and terminal integration that lets you see what the AI is doing and intervene when it goes wrong. The combination changes the interaction model completely.

The Composer Feature Changes Everything

The feature that converted me is what Cursor calls Composer. Instead of making changes one file at a time, you describe what you want to build across multiple files and it generates the complete implementation. Not just scaffolding, actual working code that you can review, modify, and ship.

I used this recently to build a small API endpoint with authentication, error handling, and test coverage in about 20 minutes. The first version was not production-ready, but it was a solid first draft that I refined rather than built from scratch. That is a fundamentally different experience from writing every line myself.

The Power User Gap

Here is what I find most interesting about Cursor’s adoption pattern. Junior developers love it. Senior developers love it even more, but for different reasons. Junior developers use it to move faster. Senior developers use it to offload boilerplate so they can focus on architecture and the genuinely hard problems.

The feedback loop is different too. When AI generates code you do not fully understand, you have two choices: accept it on faith or dig in until you do. Senior developers dig in. That means they catch errors, refine the AI’s understanding of context, and end up with better code faster. The gap between AI-assisted and unassisted productivity is probably wider for experienced developers than juniors, which is counterintuitive but makes sense when you think about where time actually goes.

The IDE Wars Are Just Getting Interesting

Cursor is winning right now, but the window is not closed. JetBrains has been moving aggressively on AI features. VS Code’s Copilot integration keeps improving. New entrants like Zed are building AI-native editors from scratch.

The interesting thing is that the models underneath are commoditising, which means the differentiation is shifting to interface design and workflow integration. That is a problem Cursor solved well for now, but it is not a permanent moat.

For developers who have not tried an AI-native editor yet, now is a good time. The productivity difference is not marginal. It is significant.

Want to read more?

Cursor’s official website

item.title: The Boring Infrastructure That Will Make or Break AI Agents
item.description:
Here is a question that nobody is asking in the current AI agent arms race: when an AI agent needs to receive a task, where does that task actually go?

Not the model’s context window. Not a shared inbox your team can see.
