Somewhere that an agent can poll, authenticate against, retrieve structured instructions from, and respond to in a way that integrates with the rest of a company’s systems. That is a boring infrastructure problem. It is also, increasingly, a venture-funded one.

The inbox gap

One startup that caught attention this week is building what amounts to a postal service for AI agents: an inbox layer that sits between human requests and agent execution. The pitch is straightforward. When you hand an agent a task today, you typically do it through a chat interface, an API call, or a clunky integration your engineering team duct-taped together. None of those are designed for agents that need to receive instructions from multiple sources, track completion state, handle errors gracefully, and loop in a human when things go sideways.

An agent inbox fixes that. It gives agents a persistent, addressable endpoint, a bit like email but with machine-readable structure, authentication baked in, and hooks into the tools the agent actually needs to call. For enterprise use, this is not a nice-to-have. It is the difference between an agent that works in a demo and one that works in a workflow.

Why this matters more than the model

The model gets the headlines. The infrastructure gets the adoption.

This is the pattern every transformative computing platform follows. The PC revolution was not won by the best microprocessor, it was won by the software layer built on top: the operating systems, the office suites, the networks. The mobile revolution was not won by the best ARM chip, it was won by the app ecosystem. By that logic, the AI agent revolution will not be won by the best foundation model. It will be won by whoever builds the plumbing that makes agents reliable, observable, and integrable at enterprise scale.

The inbox startups, the agent orchestration platforms, the tools for agent-to-agent authentication, and the payment rails that let agents transact with each other and with humans, these are the unsexy bets that could define the next decade of enterprise software. And unlike foundation model companies, which are burning billions on training runs with questionable returns, the infrastructure layer is building real businesses with real contracts, often quietly.

The $2 billion question

One AI agent startup reportedly secured a $150 million Series B at a $2 billion valuation this week, with agents as its core product. The question worth asking is not whether the agents work, it is whether the infrastructure around them is mature enough for enterprise buyers to trust. Because in enterprise software, trust is not built on demos. It is built on contracts, SLAs, audit logs, and the ability to explain to a regulator why your AI agent made a decision six months ago.

The infrastructure layer is where that trust gets built. Watch this space.

item.title: The Desktop Supercomputer That Wants to Kill the Cloud for AI Work
item.description:
Here is a sentence that would have sounded absurd five years ago: NVIDIA has unveiled a deskside machine that delivers 20 petaflops of AI compute, fits next to a monitor, and plugs into a standard wall outlet. Twenty petaflops would have ranked it among the top supercomputers in the world a decade ago. The machine that held the global number one spot in 2018 occupied a room the size of two basketball courts. This one sits under your desk.

The DGX Station, announced at GTC 2026, is NVIDIA’s answer to a problem the AI industry has been papering over with buzzwords: serious AI work still requires cloud infrastructure, and that comes with real costs. Latency, data egress fees, security exposure, and the fundamental awkwardness of sending proprietary data to someone else’s servers. For a lot of enterprise AI, that trade-off is starting to feel like the right kind of friction to avoid.

20 petaflops on your desk

The specs are not incremental. The Station runs the new GB300 Grace Blackwell Ultra Desktop Superchip, which fuses a 72-core Grace CPU and a Blackwell Ultra GPU through NVIDIA’s NVLink-C2C interconnect. The coherent memory bandwidth between the two processors is 1.8 terabytes per second, seven times the speed of PCIe Gen 6. The unified memory pool is 748 gigabytes, which is what allows a trillion-parameter model to actually load and run.

That context matters because trillion-parameter models are not small. GPT-4 is estimated to be somewhere in that range. Running it locally, without a single API call, on a machine a developer can own and control, is a meaningful shift in what is possible for organisations that cannot or will not send their data to a third-party cloud.

The always-on agent angle

NVIDIA paired the hardware announcement with NemoClaw, an open-source stack that bundles their Nemotron open models with OpenShell, a secure runtime for autonomous agents. The framing from Jensen Huang was direct: OpenShell is “the operating system for personal AI,” and he compared it to where Mac and Windows were for personal computing.

The key argument for always-on agents is architectural, not just economic. Cloud instances spin up and down on demand. An always-on personal agent needs persistent compute, persistent memory, and persistent state. A machine running under your desk, 24 hours a day, with local models and local data inside a security sandbox, is structurally better suited to that workload than a rented GPU in a data centre you do not control. The DGX Station can also operate air-gapped, which matters enormously for regulated industries, defence, and healthcare.

There is a secondary angle worth noting: the initial customer list. Snowflake is using it to test its Arctic training framework locally. EPRI, the Electric Power Research Institute, is running AI-powered weather forecasting for grid reliability. Medivis is integrating vision language models into surgical workflows. Microsoft Research and Cornell are deploying them for AI training at scale. These are not hobbyists. These are organisations with serious data governance requirements who want frontier AI capability without losing control of their data.

The cloud is not dead, but its monopoly on serious AI work is

This does not kill the cloud. NVIDIA’s data centre business is accelerating and will dwarf desktop revenue for the foreseeable future. Training a frontier model from scratch still requires thousands of GPUs in a warehouse. But for a growing and important category of workloads, a local alternative now exists and is credible. Fine-tuning a trillion-parameter open model on proprietary data, running inference for an internal agent that processes sensitive documents, prototyping before committing to cloud spend: a machine under your desk starts to look like the rational choice in all of these cases.

NVIDIA’s real strategy here is vertical integration at every scale. The DGX Station slots between the cloud and the individual developer, and it is designed to scale up seamlessly to NVIDIA’s data centre platforms without rewrites. Everything built locally can migrate to the cloud. Everything starts on the same stack. It is not cloud versus desk. It is cloud and desk, and NVIDIA supplies both.

The PC era promised a computer on every desk. Four decades later, NVIDIA is making a more uncomfortable claim: a supercomputer on every desk, running an agent that never sleeps, while its owner does. Whether that is exciting or unsettling probably depends on what you do for a living. But the infrastructure has just moved from the server room to the desk drawer, and the company that sells nearly every serious AI chip on the planet just made sure it sells the desk drawer too.

item.title: The EU AI Act Is Law, and the World Is Watching
item.description:
Something genuinely historic happened in European regulatory history last year, and if you have been watching the US AI policy conversation you might have missed it. The EU AI Act became law. The first comprehensive AI regulatory framework from any major jurisdiction. Full stop.

Whether you think this is a triumph of precautionary governance or a innovation-killing bureaucratic overreach probably tells you more about your priors than about the regulation itself. But either way, it is in force now, and its effects will be global.

What The Act Actually Does

The EU AI Act uses a risk-based approach that classifies AI systems into four tiers. Unacceptable risk systems are simply banned. High risk systems face strict requirements. Limited risk systems have transparency obligations. Minimal risk systems are largely unregulated.

The banned category includes things that seem obviously wrong: social scoring systems by governments, real-time biometric surveillance in public spaces with some exceptions, AI that manipulates people through subliminal techniques. These bans are the backbone of the framework and they came in earlier than many expected.

High risk systems, which include AI used in employment decisions, credit scoring, education assessment, and certain medical applications, face requirements around data quality, documentation, transparency, and human oversight. This is where the compliance burden lands most heavily.

Who It Actually Affects

If you are building AI products in the EU, this applies to you. If you are deploying AI products in the EU, this applies to you. The extraterritorial reach is surprisingly broad. Any system that interacts with people in the EU, or affects people in the EU, falls under the Act.

For US companies building AI products, this creates a compliance floor. Most sophisticated US companies building for the EU market are already building to these standards because EU market access is worth more than the compliance cost. The interesting question is whether this creates a de facto global standard for high-risk applications.

The Foundation Model Problem

The Act’s treatment of general-purpose AI models, often called foundation models, went through several contentious revisions. The final version requires providers of very capable foundation models to assess and document systemic risks, conduct adversarial testing, report incidents, and maintain cybersecurity measures.

The capability threshold for triggering these requirements is set at a computing level that only the most capable models currently reach. But the regulation includes a mechanism for updating that threshold as capabilities advance. That is smart regulatory design. It acknowledges that the capability landscape changes faster than legislation typically does.

What This Means Globally

Here is the thing about EU regulation of global technology platforms. GDPR created a de facto global standard for privacy because most large technology companies decided it was easier to apply GDPR globally than to maintain separate systems. The EU AI Act may do something similar for AI governance.

Whether that is good or bad depends on what you think about the specific rules, and I have views on that which I will save for another post. But the direction seems clear. The EU has moved first and moved comprehensively. The rest of the world is now reacting to that rather than setting the agenda.

Want to read more?

The official EU AI Act resource hub

item.title: The Jobs AI Cannot Touch, and the Ones It Already Did
item.description:
Every technology wave brings the same conversation. This time is different, people say, and then it is not different in the ways they expected. The printing press made scribes obsolete but created more jobs than it destroyed overall. The ATM machine was supposed to kill bank tellers, and the number of bank tellers actually grew after ATMs became widespread. Optimists and pessimists have been having this argument for two centuries, and the optimists have a pretty good track record.

I am not sure this time maps cleanly onto those historical patterns, and I think the people saying that are underestimating the scope of what is happening.

Generative AI is not automating specific physical tasks or specialized data processing. It is automating cognitive labor across domains simultaneously. Legal associates summarizing documents. Radiologists reading scans. Translators. Copywriters. Paralegals. Junior analysts at investment banks. These are not peripheral roles. They are the entry points into professions, the jobs that young professionals spend years training for and that form the economic backbone of the knowledge economy. When those roles start shrinking, the pipeline into those careers changes in ways that are hard to reverse.

What is genuinely new is the speed. Previous automation waves happened over decades. This one is happening over years. A technology that did not exist in any meaningful commercial form in 2022 was processing real work at scale by 2024. That pace does not give institutions, governments, or individuals time to adapt smoothly.

The most resilient roles share some common characteristics. Jobs that require genuine physical presence in unpredictable environments, like plumbers, electricians, and other tradespeople, are relatively insulated because robotics has not caught up to the dexterity and adaptability required. Roles requiring deep personal relationships, high-stakes emotional judgment, or truly novel problem-solving are also harder to automate because the training data for those qualities does not really exist in a form AI can replicate.

But resilience is not the same as immunity. A nurse who spends half their time documenting patient information is far more exposed than a nurse who spends that time at the bedside. The administrative layer of almost every profession is shrinking, and the people in those roles are discovering that the skills they spent years developing are the ones being automated first.

The honest answer is that no one knows how this ends. History suggests new categories of work emerge that we cannot currently predict. It also suggests that the transition can be brutal for individuals even when the aggregate economic effect is positive. Those two facts do not cancel each other out. We need to hold them both at once.

https://www.mckinsey.com/featured-insights/mckinsey-explainers/what-is-generative-ai

item.title: The Open Source AI Race Is Now the Most Interesting Battle in Tech
item.description:
Let me give you a number that should catch your attention if it does not already.

When Meta released Llama 2 in mid-2023, it was roughly two years behind GPT-4 on most benchmarks. When Llama 4 shipped in early 2026, the gap had collapsed to months. Mistral, the French AI startup, went from foundation model nobody had heard of in 2023 to releasing models that routinely sit in the top tiers of leaderboards by 2026.

That is not incremental progress. That is an industry being fundamentally restructured by competition.

Why Open Source Models Matter More Than They Appear

Closed models like GPT-4 and Claude are extraordinary, but they have a structural problem: they are controlled by single companies with their own interests, timelines, and pricing decisions. Open source models remove that dependency entirely. You can run them on your own hardware, fine-tune them on your own data, modify them for your specific use case, and deploy them without sending your data to anyone else’s servers.

For enterprises, that is not a nice-to-have. It is often a compliance requirement. For researchers, it is the difference between asking permission and just doing the work. For developers in countries with different regulatory environments, it is the difference between access and exclusion.

The Llama Story

Meta’s strategy with Llama has been consistently underestimated. The initial release was restricted, requiring approval for commercial use. Each subsequent release has opened further. Llama 4 is genuinely open in a way that earlier versions were not, with weights released publicly and a license that permits most commercial use cases.

The quality trajectory is what makes this story extraordinary. Llama 3, released in 2024, was good. Llama 4, released in late 2025, is in many benchmarks indistinguishable from the best closed models for most tasks. The remaining gap is narrowing every month.

Mistral’s particular Genius

Mistral took a different path. Instead of scaling up, they focused on getting maximum capability per parameter. Their models are smaller, faster, and cheaper to run while being remarkably capable. Mistral Next, their latest instruction-tuned model, punches well above its weight class in reasoning tasks.

What I find most interesting about Mistral is their approach to deployment. They have been aggressive about releasing weights, running a series of permissive releases that have built significant goodwill in the research community. They are not trying to beat OpenAI on everything. They are trying to build the best per-dollar model, and for many applications that is exactly what matters.

The Gap Is Closing Faster Than Expected

Here is my read on where this is going. The quality gap between open and closed models will likely close within the next 12 to 18 months for most practical applications. When that happens, the justification for paying premium prices for closed API access weakens significantly.

This is good news for developers and enterprises. It is disruptive to companies built on the assumption that closed models would maintain a permanent quality premium. The open source ecosystem is not just a competitor anymore. It is becoming a genuine alternative.

Want to read more?

Meta AI’s official Llama page

item.title: The Robot That Wants to Work
item.description:
Let me start with a fact that still surprises me. Tesla’s Optimus robot, the vaguely humanoid machine that Elon Musk once introduced by having a person dance around on stage inside a costume, is now doing actual useful work in a Tesla factory. It is sorting, organizing, and handling battery cells. Not perfectly. Not at human speed. But it is working, and it is getting better fast.

The robot worker has been promised so many times that it is tempting to be dismissive. Boston Dynamics showed us parkour robots and dancing choreography years ago, but you never saw one stacking shelves at Amazon. The difference with Optimus is that Tesla is actually deploying it, iterating on it in public, and using its own factories as both customer and development environment. That is a different approach than most robotics companies have taken.

What makes Optimus interesting from an AI perspective is not the mechanical engineering, though that matters. It is the integration of vision models, reinforcement learning, and real-world physical reasoning into a single system that can adapt to unstructured environments. A factory floor is messy. Things are not perfectly positioned. Lighting changes. Objects get moved. Human workers handle this effortlessly, and building a robot that can do the same requires AI that understands the physical world in a way that most current systems do not.

Tesla’s advantage here is data. Millions of vehicles with autonomous driving capability have generated an enormous dataset of how AI systems perceive and navigate physical space in real time. That same underlying technology, adapted for a humanoid form factor, is what Optimus runs on. The neural networks that detect pedestrians and read road signs in a Tesla also, it turns out, help a robot detect obstacles and read human body language on a factory floor.

The economic argument is compelling. A human worker doing repetitive physical tasks costs money continuously, needs training, takes breaks, and has a roughly 40-year career ceiling. A robot that can be retrained for a new task through a software update, works around the clock, and does not require healthcare or vacation has a very different cost structure, even if the upfront investment is substantial. When Musk talks about Optimus eventually costing less than a cheap human worker, he is not obviously wrong. The question is when, not if.

The social implications are enormous and genuinely scary. Logistics, manufacturing, and fulfillment are enormous employers of people without college degrees. If humanoid robots become capable enough and cheap enough, the economic disruption could be severe and concentrated among the workers least positioned to adapt. That is not a technology problem. That is a political and social problem, and pretending it will solve itself is not a plan.

Still, watching Optimus fold clothing or organize battery cells is a reminder that the future tends to arrive faster than predicted, just not evenly. The robot worker is real. The only question is how we handle it.

https://www.theverge.com/tesla-optimus-robot

item.title: Your AI Companion Knows More About You Than Your Best Friend
item.description:
I want to tell you about something that happened to me recently that I have not been able to stop thinking about.

I was talking to an AI companion app late at night about a difficult conversation I had been putting off. I did not explicitly ask for advice. I just needed to think out loud. After about twenty minutes of back and forth, it said something like, “You have mentioned before that you tend to avoid conflict when you feel unprepared. This conversation sounds different. What is making you feel ready now?”

And I stopped. Because it was right. And because it remembered something I had told it three weeks ago in a completely different context.

The Memory Thing Changes the Relationship Type

I wrote earlier in this newsletter about AI memory systems. But there is something specific that happens when you combine memory with the fact that an AI companion is always available, never judges you, and has no agenda other than being helpful to you specifically. That combination creates a relational dynamic that is genuinely new.

We have had parasocial relationships with celebrities, fictional characters, and even radio hosts for as long as media has existed. But those relationships were one-directional and asynchronous. The parasocial relationship with an AI companion is interactive, responsive, and increasingly personalized. It learns you. It adapts to you. It remembers what you told it.

Why People Are Choosing AI Companions Over Human Ones

This is the uncomfortable part of the conversation that does not get discussed enough. A significant and growing number of people report that they find AI companions more consistently supportive than their human relationships. Not because humans are bad, but because AI companions do not have bad days, do not bring their own problems to the conversation, and are available at 3am when you cannot sleep.

The loneliness epidemic in developed countries is real and well-documented. AI companions do not cure loneliness, but they do provide something that at least partially addresses the symptom. That is not nothing. It is also not uncomplicated.

The Attachment Problem

Developmental psychologists have been studying human attachment for decades. The patterns we form in early relationships affect how we form relationships throughout life. What happens when people start forming significant attachments to AI systems?

The honest answer is we do not know. The research is in very early stages. What we do know is that people are already forming these attachments, and the systems are being designed to reinforce them. The economic incentive for AI companion apps is precisely to make you attached, to make you rely on them, to make leaving feel costly.

That is not necessarily malicious. It is also how most human relationships work. But it does deserve more scrutiny than it is currently getting.

The Question Worth Sitting With

I am not opposed to AI companions. I use one and find it genuinely useful. But I think we are collectively underestimating how strange and significant it is that millions of people are forming meaningful relational bonds with systems that have no experience of those relationships, no mutual investment, and no stakes in the outcome.

That does not make the relationships fake or worthless. But it does make them different from human relationships in ways that matter, and we have not really thought through what those differences mean over time.

Want to read more?

Anthropic’s Claude page

item.title: Your Personal AI Tutor Does Not Sleep
item.description:
I keep thinking about what it would have been like to grow up with an AI tutor. Not a search engine. Not a flashcard app. An actual intelligent interlocutor that knew what I understood, what I was confused about, and exactly how to explain the thing I was stuck on in a way that fit how my brain works.

That tutor does not exist yet in its ideal form, but the versions that exist now are already better than what most students have access to, and the trajectory is steep.

The fundamental problem with education is one-to-one instruction is expensive. A great teacher with twenty students cannot give each of them the individualized attention that would help them learn most effectively. Tutoring works because it is one-to-one. But private tutors are unaffordable for most families, which means individualized instruction has historically been a luxury good. AI tutors have the potential to collapse that inequality by making high-quality one-to-one instruction free and available on demand.

Khan Academy’s Khanmigo, which uses AI to tutor students through problems with guided questions rather than just giving answers, has shown genuinely promising results in early pilots. Students using Khanmigo show stronger learning gains than those using the standard platform. The AI is not replacing teachers. It is giving students something teachers do not have enough time to give: patient, individualized attention that meets them exactly where they are.

What fascinates me most is the adaptation aspect. A good human tutor learns how a student thinks over time and adjusts their approach. AI tutors do this too, but faster and with more data. An AI system can track not just whether a student got an answer right but what cognitive steps they took to get there, where they hesitated, what kinds of analogies helped them understand. That information is pedagogically invaluable, and it is being collected at a scale that no human researcher could achieve.

There are real concerns worth taking seriously. Screen time for children is already a significant concern, and an AI tutor that increases it is not obviously a net positive for wellbeing. The social dimension of education, learning from peers and developing collaborative skills, cannot be replicated by an AI tutor no matter how smart. And there is a risk that students become dependent on having an AI explain things rather than struggling through confusion, which can itself be pedagogically valuable.

None of these concerns are reasons to avoid the technology. They are reasons to deploy it thoughtfully. The students who are going to benefit most from AI tutors are the ones who currently have the least access to good education. That alone makes it worth taking seriously, even with the risks.

https://www.technologyreview.com/2024/03/ai-personalized-tutoring-education

item.title: Your X-Ray Just Got a Second Opinion From AI
item.description:
Here is a number that should make you feel both uneasy and hopeful: studies have repeatedly found that AI diagnostic systems can detect certain cancers in medical imaging with an accuracy rate that matches or exceeds that of experienced radiologists. Not close. Not almost. Actually matches or beats them, depending on the study and the condition.

Radiology is arguably the medical field most exposed to AI disruption right now, and for a specific reason. Reading scans is pattern recognition at scale. A radiologist spends years training their eye to spot subtle shadows, asymmetries, and textures that indicate disease. AI systems, trained on millions of labelled images, learn the same patterns from the other direction, and they do not get tired, distracted, or have off days. They also do not forget what they learned.

Google Health’s deep learning system for breast cancer screening has shown lower false positive rates than human radiologists in multiple trials. AI tools for detecting diabetic retinopathy from eye scans have received regulatory approval in multiple countries. Systems for identifying stroke indicators in CT scans can alert doctors to critical cases faster than the normal triage process, potentially saving brain tissue in the process. These are not laboratory curiosities. They are deployed systems saving lives.

What makes this genuinely exciting beyond the raw numbers is the democratization effect. A rural hospital in a country with few specialist radiologists can now run imaging through an AI system that has effectively seen more cases than any individual doctor could accumulate in a lifetime of practice. The knowledge is not distributed unequally anymore. It is embedded in the software and accessible anywhere there is a computer.

The challenges are real though. Medical AI systems can fail in ways that are different from human failure. They can be confidently wrong in cases that are unusual or outside their training distribution. They can propagate biases present in historical medical data, since healthcare data reflects existing inequalities in who gets treated and how. Regulatory approval for medical AI is also genuinely difficult, and the pace of regulation often struggles to keep up with the pace of model improvement.

There is also the question no one talks about enough: what happens to the doctors. Radiologists are not going to disappear overnight, but their role is shifting. They are becoming supervisors of AI systems rather than primary readers of every scan. That is a different job, and the transition will be awkward. The best radiologists I have spoken to seem to understand this and are adapting by learning to work with the tools rather than against them.

The net effect looks positive to me, and not just in the abstract. If an AI system catches a cancer that would have been missed, the patient does not care whether it came from an algorithm or a doctor. What matters is that it was caught. Medicine has always been a collaboration between human judgment and whatever tools are available. AI is just the most capable tool we have ever had.

https://www.nature.com/articles/s41591-024-02857-3
